expanded_description: Line segments are measurable portions of lines with definite endpoints. Understanding how to measure, compare, and calculate segment lengths using the Segment Addition Postulate is essential for solving geometric problems involving distance, perimeter, and coordinate geometry.

core_concept.principle: A line segment is a finite portion of a line bounded by two endpoints. Every segment has a measurable length, and the Segment Addition Postulate allows us to calculate total length by adding the lengths of its parts when a point lies between the endpoints.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a segment as a straight stick with two ends. You can measure exactly how long that stick is. If you break the stick at any point along its length, the two pieces add up to the original length. This simple idea—the whole equals the sum of its parts—is the Segment Addition Postulate, and it is one of the most practical tools in all of geometry.

image.alt: A segment AB shown as a horizontal stick with endpoints A and B. A point C lies between A and B, dividing the stick into two pieces labeled AC and CB. Below: arrows showing AC + CB = AB, with the caption 'The whole equals the sum of its parts.'
image.caption: Segment Addition Postulate: If point C lies between A and B, then AC + CB = AB.

sections.introduction: A line segment is the most basic measurable object in geometry. Unlike lines and rays, segments have finite length, making them essential for any discussion of distance, size, and perimeter.

introduction_image.alt: Three panels comparing: Line AB with arrows on both ends (infinite), Ray AB with one arrow (infinite in one direction), Segment AB with two endpoints and no arrows (finite, measurable).
introduction_image.caption: Comparison: A line extends infinitely, a ray has one endpoint, a segment has two endpoints and a measurable length.

key_concept.definition: A line segment is the part of a line consisting of two distinct endpoints and all points between them. The length of a segment is the distance between its endpoints, expressed in units of measurement.
key_concept.context: Segments are the building blocks of polygons—every side of a triangle, rectangle, or any polygon is a line segment. Measuring segments is the first step toward calculating perimeter, area, and working with coordinate geometry.

image.alt: Diagram of segment AB. Endpoints A and B are labeled with large dots. The segment is highlighted. Below: notation key — 'AB with bar = the segment (geometric figure)', 'AB without bar = the length (a number, e.g., 8 cm)'.
image.caption: Segment Notation: AB— is the geometric figure; AB is its numerical length.

process_flow.process: Identify the Segment and Its Endpoints
process_flow.description: Locate the two points that define the segment. The segment includes both endpoints and every point between them.
process_flow.mechanism: A segment is named by its two endpoints with a bar over the letters. Segment AB (written AB with a horizontal bar) has endpoints A and B. The order of letters does not matter: AB— is the same as BA—.
process_flow.result: Clear identification of the geometric object and its boundaries.
applications: Segment PQ has endpoints P and Q
Segment XY— is identical to Segment YX—
Every side of polygon ABCD is a segment

image.alt: Several segments drawn: AB with endpoints highlighted, PQ on a polygon side, XY shown with bar notation. Label: 'AB— = BA— (order does not matter).'
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the segment by its two endpoints. Order of naming does not change the segment.

process_flow.description: The length of a segment is the distance between its endpoints, denoted by the endpoint letters without a bar.
process_flow.mechanism: AB (without a bar) means 'the length of segment AB.' This is a numerical value with units (inches, centimeters, etc.) or a variable expression in algebra problems. Length is always a positive number.
process_flow.result: A numerical or algebraic representation of the segment's size.
applications: If AB = 5 cm, the distance from A to B is 5 centimeters
If PQ = x + 3, the length is expressed in terms of x
Length is always positive: AB > 0 for distinct points

image.alt: Segment AB shown with a ruler below it, marking 0 at A and 8 at B. Caption: 'AB = 8 cm means the distance from A to B is 8 centimeters.'
image.caption: Step 2: Length is the distance between endpoints, always a positive number with units.

process_flow.description: Segments can be compared using their lengths. Congruent segments have equal lengths.
process_flow.mechanism: If AB = CD, then segment AB is congruent to segment CD, written AB— ≅ CD—. Congruence means 'same size and shape'—for segments, this simply means same length. The symbol ≅ means 'is congruent to.'
process_flow.result: Ability to identify equal-length segments and use congruence in reasoning.
applications: If AB = 7 and CD = 7, then AB— ≅ CD—
In an equilateral triangle, all three sides are congruent
Midpoint of a segment creates two congruent segments

image.alt: Two segments AB and CD shown in different locations, both measuring 7 cm. Matching single tick marks on both. Notation: 'AB— ≅ CD— because AB = CD = 7 cm.'
image.caption: Step 3: Congruent segments have equal length. Matching tick marks indicate congruence in diagrams.

classifications.value: Standard ways to write and refer to segments and their lengths.
characteristics: AB with a bar (AB—) refers to the segment itself—the geometric figure
AB without a bar refers to the length—a number
The congruence symbol ≅ compares segments; the equals sign = compares lengths
Segments are labeled with capital letters at endpoints
classifications.behavior: Precise notation distinguishes the geometric object (segment) from its measurement (length). Using the correct notation is essential for clear communication in proofs and problem-solving.

examples.explanation:
'Draw AB—' means draw the segment.
'AB = 8 cm' means the length is 8 centimeters.
'AB— ≅ CD—' means the segments are congruent (same length).

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison: Left — segment AB drawn with bar above, labeled 'AB— (segment, a figure)'. Center — 'AB = 8 cm (length, a number)'. Right — two segments with matching tick marks, labeled 'AB— ≅ CD— (congruent figures)'.
image.caption: Notation key: Bar = figure, no bar = number, ≅ = congruence.

examples.explanation:
Small matching tick marks on segments in a diagram indicate congruence.
Single tick, double tick, triple tick distinguish different pairs of congruent segments.

image.alt: Three pairs of segments: one pair with single ticks, one pair with double ticks, one pair with triple ticks. Caption: 'Single ticks match single ticks; double match double; they do not cross-match.'
image.caption: Tick mark convention: Matching marks indicate congruent segments.

classifications.value: A point is between two other points if it lies on the segment connecting them, requiring collinearity.
characteristics: Point B is between A and C if A, B, C are collinear and AB + BC = AC
'Between' implies collinearity—the three points must lie on the same line
Exactly one point can be between the other two when three points are collinear
Betweenness is the key condition for applying the Segment Addition Postulate
classifications.behavior: Betweenness is a precise geometric relationship. If three points are not collinear (forming a triangle), no point is 'between' the other two in the geometric sense.

examples.explanation:
Points A, B, C on a line with B between A and C.
Then AB + BC = AC, and B is the unique point between A and C.

image.alt: A horizontal line with points A, B, C in order. B is labeled 'B is between A and C'. AB and BC are highlighted in different colors. Equation below: AB + BC = AC.
image.caption: Betweenness: B lies on segment AC, so AB + BC = AC.

examples.explanation: Points D, E, F forming a triangle—no point is between the other two because they are not collinear. Betweenness requires all points on one line.

image.alt: Triangle DEF with vertices labeled. A red X over the label 'B is between...?' indicating the concept does not apply to noncollinear points.
image.caption: Non-example: In a triangle, no vertex is 'between' the other two—they are not collinear.

image.alt: Overview diagram: Segment AB with length marked, a ruler showing measurement, congruent segments with tick marks, and a collinear set A-B-C illustrating betweenness. Comprehensive visual summary of the section.
image.caption: Understanding Segments: Notation, measurement, congruence, and betweenness.

sections.introduction: The Segment Addition Postulate is a fundamental rule that connects the lengths of adjacent segments on a line. It formalizes the intuitive idea that the whole equals the sum of its parts and is used constantly in geometric problem-solving.

introduction_image.alt: A segment AC with point B between A and C. The segment is visually split into two colored parts: AB in blue and BC in red. Equation below: AB + BC = AC. A ruler along the bottom confirms the sum.
introduction_image.caption: The Segment Addition Postulate: The length of the whole segment equals the sum of its two parts.

key_concept.definition: If point B lies between points A and C on a line, then AB + BC = AC. In words: the length of the whole segment equals the sum of the lengths of the two smaller segments.
key_concept.context: This postulate is the foundation for solving countless geometry problems—finding unknown lengths, locating midpoints, setting up algebraic equations, and proving more advanced theorems about triangles and polygons.

image.alt: Clean diagram: Segment AC with B between A and C. Three equations shown: AB + BC = AC, AC - AB = BC, AC - BC = AB. Visual arrows indicate which part corresponds to which equation.
image.caption: Segment Addition Postulate and its rearrangements: whole minus one part equals the other part.

classifications.value: If B is between A and C, then AB + BC = AC.
characteristics: Requires B to be between A and C (collinear and lying on segment AC)
The sum of the two parts equals the whole
Works with numbers, variables, and algebraic expressions
Can be rearranged: AC - AB = BC or AC - BC = AB
classifications.behavior: The postulate lets you find any one of the three lengths if you know the other two. Add parts to get the whole; subtract a part from the whole to get the other part.

examples.explanation:
If AB = 5 cm and BC = 7 cm, with B between A and C:
AC = AB + BC = 5 + 7 = 12 cm

image.alt: Segment diagram: AB = 5 cm (blue), BC = 7 cm (red), B between A and C. Calculation: AC = 5 + 7 = 12 cm. The full segment AC is labeled 12 cm.
image.caption: Finding total length: Add the two parts to get the whole.

examples.explanation:
If AC = 20 m and AB = 8 m, with B between A and C:
BC = AC - AB = 20 - 8 = 12 m

image.alt: Segment diagram: AC = 20 m (whole), AB = 8 m (known part). BC is marked with a question mark. Calculation: BC = 20 - 8 = 12 m. BC labeled 12 m.
image.caption: Finding a missing part: Subtract the known part from the whole.

classifications.value: The postulate is used to set up and solve equations when segment lengths involve variables.
characteristics: Lengths may be given as algebraic expressions (e.g., 2x + 1, 3x - 4)
Set up equation: (expression for AB) + (expression for BC) = (expression for AC)
Solve for the variable, then substitute back to find actual lengths
Always check that lengths are positive after solving
classifications.behavior: The Segment Addition Postulate transforms geometric relationships into algebraic equations that can be solved systematically using standard algebra techniques.

examples.process: Solving for x and finding lengths
examples.explanation:
AB = 2x, BC = 3x + 1, AC = 21. B is between A and C.
Equation: 2x + (3x + 1) = 21
5x + 1 = 21 → 5x = 20 → x = 4
Then AB = 2(4) = 8, BC = 3(4) + 1 = 13
Check: 8 + 13 = 21 ✓

image.alt: Segment AC with B between A and C. Labels: AB = 2x, BC = 3x + 1, AC = 21. Work shown step-by-step: equation setup, solving for x = 4, substituting back to find AB = 8 and BC = 13.
image.caption: Algebraic application: Set up the equation, solve for the variable, and find all lengths.

worked_examples.title: Applying the Segment Addition Postulate
worked_examples.problem:
Point B lies between A and C.
1) AB = 6, BC = 11. Find AC.
2) AC = 30, AB = 12. Find BC.
3) AB = 2x - 3, BC = x + 7, AC = 4x. Find x and all three lengths.

step_by_step.explanation:
Since B is between A and C, apply the postulate:
AC = AB + BC
AC = 6 + 11 = 17

Substitute the expressions into AB + BC = AC:
(2x - 3) + (x + 7) = 4x
3x + 4 = 4x

image.alt: Three sub-diagrams: (1) AB = 6, BC = 11, AC = 17. (2) AC = 30, AB = 12, BC = 18. (3) Segment with algebraic expressions AB = 2x - 3, BC = x + 7, AC = 4x, and final lengths AB = 5, BC = 11, AC = 16.
image.caption: Worked examples: Finding total length, missing part, and solving with variables.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Segment Addition Postulate: If B is between A and C, then AB + BC = AC. Used to find total length, missing parts, and solve algebraic equations.

sections.introduction: When segments have equal length, they are congruent. A midpoint divides a segment into two congruent segments. These concepts combine the Segment Addition Postulate with the idea of equality to solve problems about symmetry and bisection.

introduction_image.alt: Left: Two separate congruent segments AB and CD with matching tick marks. Right: A single segment PQ with midpoint M, showing PM and MQ with matching tick marks and equal length labels.
introduction_image.caption: Congruent segments have equal length. A midpoint splits a segment into two congruent parts.

key_concept.definition: Congruent segments are segments that have the same length, denoted by the symbol ≅. A midpoint of a segment is the point that divides the segment into two congruent segments—it lies exactly halfway between the endpoints.
key_concept.context: Congruence is a fundamental concept throughout geometry. Midpoints are used to construct perpendicular bisectors, medians of triangles, and to find the center of line segments in coordinate geometry.

image.alt: Segment AB with midpoint M labeled. AM and MB are highlighted in different colors. Equations: AM = MB, AM + MB = AB, therefore AM = MB = AB ÷ 2. M is exactly halfway between A and B.
image.caption: Midpoint M: AM = MB, and AM + MB = AB, so AM = MB = AB/2.

classifications.value: Segments with equal length; the geometric figures have the same size.
characteristics: AB— ≅ CD— means the segments are congruent (same length)
If AB— ≅ CD—, then AB = CD (equal numerical lengths)
Congruence is about the figure; equality is about the number
In diagrams, matching tick marks indicate congruence
classifications.behavior: Congruent segments can be in different locations but have identical measurements. Congruence is an equivalence relation—it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

examples.explanation:
If AB = 5 and CD = 5, then AB— ≅ CD—.
The segments have the same length, so they are congruent figures.

image.alt: Two segments in different positions: AB = 5 cm with single tick, CD = 5 cm with single tick. Statement: AB— ≅ CD— because AB = CD = 5 cm.
image.caption: Segments with equal length are congruent, regardless of position.

examples.explanation:
Two segments each marked with a single tick are congruent to each other.
Segments with double ticks are congruent to each other but not necessarily to single-ticked segments.

image.alt: A triangle with sides marked: two sides with single ticks (congruent to each other), one side with double ticks (different length). Caption explains tick mark grouping.
image.caption: Tick marks group congruent segments: single ticks match single ticks; double ticks match double ticks.

classifications.value: The point on a segment that divides it into two congruent segments.
characteristics: If M is the midpoint of AB—, then AM— ≅ MB— (so AM = MB)
M lies between A and B, so AM + MB = AB
Since AM = MB, we have AM = MB = AB ÷ 2
Every segment has exactly one midpoint
classifications.behavior: The midpoint is the unique point equidistant from both endpoints. It splits the segment exactly in half. Finding midpoints is essential for bisection and symmetry.

examples.explanation:
If M is the midpoint of AB— and AB = 14 cm, then
AM = MB = 14 ÷ 2 = 7 cm

image.alt: Segment AB = 14 cm. Midpoint M marked. AM = 7 cm, MB = 7 cm. Calculation: 14 ÷ 2 = 7 cm for each half.
image.caption: When total length is known, each half is the total divided by 2.

examples.explanation:
M is midpoint of PQ—. PM = 3x + 1, MQ = 5x - 7.
Since M is midpoint, PM = MQ.
3x + 1 = 5x - 7 → 8 = 2x → x = 4
PM = 3(4) + 1 = 13, MQ = 5(4) - 7 = 13
PQ = PM + MQ = 26

image.alt: Segment PQ with midpoint M. PM = 3x + 1, MQ = 5x - 7. Equation: 3x + 1 = 5x - 7. Work shown: 8 = 2x, x = 4. Final: PM = 13, MQ = 13, PQ = 26.
image.caption: Algebraic midpoint problem: Set the two halves equal and solve for x.

worked_examples.title: Midpoint and Congruence Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) M is the midpoint of XY—. If XM = 9, find MY and XY.
2) B is the midpoint of AC—. AB = 2x + 3, BC = 5x - 9. Find x, AB, BC, and AC.
3) In the diagram, PQ— ≅ QR— and QR— ≅ RS—. If PQ = 4, find PS.

step_by_step.explanation:
Since M is the midpoint of XY—, XM = MY.
MY = 9 (same as XM)
XY = XM + MY = 9 + 9 = 18

B is the midpoint, so AB = BC.
2x + 3 = 5x - 9

PQ— ≅ QR— means PQ = QR = 4
QR— ≅ RS— means QR = RS = 4
Points P, Q, R, S are collinear and in order.
PS = PQ + QR + RS = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) Segment XY with midpoint M, XM = MY = 9, XY = 18. (2) Segment AC with midpoint B, AB = BC = 11, AC = 22. (3) Collinear points P, Q, R, S with congruent adjacent segments, each 4 units, PS = 12.
image.caption: Midpoint and congruence problems: Using equality of halves and congruence chains.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Midpoint divides a segment into two congruent segments (equal lengths). Congruent segments have equal lengths. Segment Addition Postulate extends to multiple collinear segments.

sections.introduction: A segment bisector is any geometric figure—a point, line, ray, or plane—that passes through the midpoint of a segment. Understanding bisectors connects the concepts of midpoints, congruence, and the Segment Addition Postulate.

introduction_image.alt: Four panels showing a segment AB with midpoint M: (1) Point M alone labeled 'point bisector', (2) A line passing through M, (3) A ray starting at M, (4) A shaded plane containing M. All labeled as valid bisectors.
introduction_image.caption: Any geometric figure passing through the midpoint bisects the segment.

key_concept.definition: A segment bisector is a point, line, ray, segment, or plane that intersects a segment at its midpoint, dividing the segment into two congruent segments.
key_concept.context: Bisectors are fundamental in geometric constructions, symmetry, and proofs. The perpendicular bisector of a segment is especially important in later topics involving triangles, circles, and coordinate geometry.

image.alt: Left: Segment AB with midpoint M. A slanted line through M labeled 'bisector' (any angle). Right: Segment AB with midpoint M. A vertical line through M forming a right angle marked with a square. Labeled 'perpendicular bisector'.
image.caption: Any line through the midpoint is a bisector. A perpendicular bisector also forms a 90° angle.

classifications.value: Any geometric figure passing through the midpoint of a segment can bisect it.
characteristics: A point at the midpoint bisects the segment into two congruent segments
A line passing through the midpoint bisects the segment
A ray with endpoint at the midpoint (extending in either direction) bisects the segment
Another segment crossing at the midpoint bisects the segment
A plane containing the midpoint (in 3D) bisects the segment
classifications.behavior: The bisector must pass through the midpoint. It does not need to be perpendicular—any line through the midpoint is a bisector. The perpendicular bisector is a special and important case.

examples.explanation:
Line ℓ passes through M, the midpoint of AB—.
Line ℓ is a segment bisector of AB—.
AM— ≅ MB—.

image.alt: Segment AB with midpoint M. A slanted line ℓ passes through M. AM and MB have matching tick marks. Label: 'Line ℓ bisects AB— because it passes through midpoint M.'
image.caption: Line ℓ is a segment bisector—it passes through the midpoint M.

examples.explanation:
Ray MX has endpoint at M, the midpoint of AB—.
Ray MX bisects AB— because it passes through the midpoint.

image.alt: Segment AB with midpoint M. A ray MX starts at M and extends upward. AM and MB have matching tick marks. Label: 'Ray MX bisects AB—.'
image.caption: Ray MX bisects the segment because its endpoint M is the midpoint.

classifications.value: A bisector that intersects the segment at a 90° angle at its midpoint.
characteristics: Passes through the midpoint (bisects the segment)
Forms a right angle (90°) with the segment
Every point on the perpendicular bisector is equidistant from the segment's endpoints
Unique to a given segment—there is exactly one perpendicular bisector
classifications.behavior: The perpendicular bisector is the most important type of segment bisector. It combines the properties of midpoint and perpendicularity. It is the line of symmetry for the segment.

examples.explanation:
Line ℓ passes through midpoint M of AB— and is perpendicular to AB—.
Line ℓ is the perpendicular bisector of AB—.
Any point P on ℓ has PA = PB (equidistant from endpoints).

image.alt: Segment AB with midpoint M. A vertical line passes through M with a right angle square marked. Point P is on the line above M. Dashed lines connect P to A and P to B. Labels: PA = PB, AM = MB, right angle marked.
image.caption: Perpendicular bisector: passes through midpoint at 90°. Points on it are equidistant from A and B.

image.alt: Comprehensive diagram: Segment AB with midpoint M. Shown: a point on M (bisector), a slanted line through M (bisector), a ray from M (bisector), and a perpendicular line through M with right angle (perpendicular bisector). All labeled clearly.
image.caption: Segment Bisectors: Any figure through the midpoint bisects; the perpendicular bisector is a special right-angle case.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes with Segments and Length
sections.introduction: Several errors commonly occur when working with segment notation, the Segment Addition Postulate, and midpoint problems. Identifying these patterns helps avoid losing points on problems you otherwise understand.

key_concept.definition: Recognizing and avoiding common errors—notation confusion, missing collinearity conditions, misidentifying midpoints, and accepting negative lengths—is essential for accurate geometric reasoning and problem-solving.
key_concept.context: These errors stem from misunderstanding the precise language and conditions of geometry. Mastering the details of notation, betweenness, and midpoint conditions prevents these mistakes.

classifications.value: Using AB— (segment) and AB (length) interchangeably, or using the wrong symbol for comparison.
characteristics: Error: Writing 'AB— = 5 cm' (should be 'AB = 5 cm' — a figure cannot equal a number)
Error: Writing 'AB ≅ CD' (should be 'AB— ≅ CD—' — congruence compares figures, not numbers)
Correct: AB— is the figure; AB is the number; ≅ compares figures; = compares numbers
classifications.behavior: The bar over letters denotes the geometric object. Without the bar, it denotes the length. Mixing these creates statements that are technically meaningless.

examples.explanation:
Incorrect: AB— = 8 cm (segment equals a number)
Correct: AB = 8 cm (length equals a number)
Incorrect: AB ≅ CD (lengths are congruent)
Correct: AB— ≅ CD— (segments are congruent)
Also correct: AB = CD (lengths are equal)

image.alt: Two-column comparison: 'Incorrect' column showing AB— = 5 cm with red X, and 'Correct' column showing AB = 5 cm with green check. Similar pairs for congruence notation.
image.caption: Notation precision: Bar = figure, no bar = number, ≅ = congruence of figures, = = equality of numbers.

classifications.note: Solution: Bar = geometric figure. No bar = length (number). Congruence symbol ≅ = figures have same size. Equals sign = numbers have same value. Say it aloud: 'Segment AB' means the figure; 'length AB' means the number.

classifications.value: Applying the Segment Addition Postulate when the point is not between the endpoints on the same line.
characteristics: Error: Writing AB + BC = AC when B is not on segment AC
Error: Assuming three points are collinear without being told or shown so
Correct: The postulate only applies if B is between A and C on the same line
classifications.behavior: The Segment Addition Postulate requires betweenness, which requires collinearity. If the three points form a triangle, the postulate does not apply—you must use the Triangle Inequality instead.

examples.explanation:
Points A, B, C form a triangle. AB = 3, BC = 4, AC = 5.
It would be wrong to write AB + BC = AC because 3 + 4 ≠ 5, and B is not between A and C on a line. The Triangle Inequality says AB + BC > AC (3 + 4 > 5).

image.alt: Triangle ABC with sides 3, 4, 5. A red X over the equation 'AB + BC = AC' (3 + 4 = 5, false). A green check over 'AB + BC > AC' (3 + 4 > 5, true). Caption: 'Segment Addition requires collinearity; for triangles use the Triangle Inequality.'
image.caption: Segment Addition Postulate requires collinear points. In a triangle, the sum of two sides is greater than the third.

classifications.note: Solution: Verify that the middle point lies on the segment connecting the outer points. Look for the word 'between' or a diagram showing collinearity. If the points form a triangle, the Segment Addition Postulate does not apply.

classifications.value: Setting up the wrong equation for midpoint problems by confusing the postulate with the midpoint property.
characteristics: Error: Writing only AM + MB = AB and thinking that's the midpoint condition (it's always true when M is between A and B)
Error: Writing AM = AB/2 without first establishing M is the midpoint
Correct: The midpoint condition is AM = MB. Combine with AM + MB = AB to get AM = MB = AB/2.
classifications.behavior: The Segment Addition Postulate (AM + MB = AB) is true whenever M is between A and B. The midpoint adds the specific condition that AM = MB. Both facts are needed for a complete solution.

examples.explanation:
M is midpoint of AB—.
Equation 1 (always true when M is between A and B): AM + MB = AB
Equation 2 (the midpoint condition): AM = MB
Substituting (2) into (1): AM + AM = AB → 2(AM) = AB → AM = AB/2

image.alt: Segment AB with midpoint M. Two equations highlighted: 'AM + MB = AB (always true if M is between A and B)' and 'AM = MB (this is the specific midpoint condition)'. Both equations are needed.
image.caption: The midpoint condition (AM = MB) is separate from the Segment Addition Postulate (AM + MB = AB).

classifications.note: Solution: For midpoints, use AM = MB as your starting equation, or use AM = AB/2 if you know the total length. The postulate AM + MB = AB is the supporting fact, not the defining condition.

classifications.value: Keeping a solution that produces a negative or zero value as a valid segment length.
characteristics: Error: Getting x = -2 and reporting AB = -4 as a valid length
Error: Not rejecting solutions that give zero or negative lengths
Correct: Length must be positive. Reject any solution that produces a non-positive length.
classifications.behavior: Segment length is a distance—it is always a positive number. If solving an equation gives a value of x that makes a length zero or negative, that value must be rejected or the problem must be re-examined.

examples.explanation:
AB = 2x - 10, and solving yields x = 3.
Then AB = 2(3) - 10 = -4.
A length of -4 is impossible. Either the equation was set up incorrectly, or x = 3 is an extraneous solution. Re-examine the problem.

image.alt: Algebraic work: AB = 2x - 10, x = 3, so AB = -4. A red X over -4. Note: 'Length cannot be negative. Reject or re-examine.' A ruler showing distance is always positive.
image.caption: Length must be positive. Negative values indicate an error in setup or an invalid solution.

classifications.note: Solution: After solving for a variable, always substitute back to find actual lengths. Verify every length is positive (> 0). If not, check your equation setup or reject that solution.

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Segment Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student attempts these problems. Identify and correct each error.
1) Student writes: 'AB— = 10 cm'.
2) Points P, Q, R form a triangle. Student writes: PQ + QR = PR.
3) M is midpoint of XY—. Student writes: XM + MY = XY, therefore XM = XY/2, without stating XM = MY.

step_by_step.explanation:
Error: AB— is the segment (a geometric figure). It cannot equal a number.
Correct: AB = 10 cm (the length equals the number). Or state: 'The length of AB— is 10 cm.'

Error: The Segment Addition Postulate requires Q to be between P and R on a line. In a triangle, no point is between the other two—they are noncollinear.
Correct: For triangle PQR, the Triangle Inequality states PQ + QR > PR (the sum of any two sides is greater than the third).

While the conclusion XM = XY/2 is correct, the reasoning skipped a step. The Segment Addition Postulate gives XM + MY = XY. The midpoint condition gives XM = MY. Substituting: XM + XM = XY → 2(XM) = XY → XM = XY/2. Both equations are needed for a complete argument.

image.alt: Three panels: (1) AB— with red X, corrected to AB = 10 cm with green check. (2) Triangle PQR with red X over PQ + QR = PR, corrected to PQ + QR > PR. (3) Segment XY with midpoint M, both equations XM + MY = XY and XM = MY shown with green checks.
image.caption: Corrected errors: Precise notation, collinearity requirement, and complete midpoint reasoning.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) AB = 10 cm (length, no bar)
2) For a triangle, PQ + QR > PR (Triangle Inequality), not equal
3) XM = MY is the midpoint condition; combine with XM + MY = XY to get XM = XY/2
worked_examples.concept_applied: Notation: bar vs. no bar. Segment Addition Postulate requires collinearity and betweenness. Midpoint requires both the postulate and the equality condition.

sections.introduction: Measuring and calculating segment lengths is one of the most practical skills in geometry, with applications spanning construction, navigation, design, and everyday problem-solving.

categories.description: Builders use segment measurement and the Segment Addition Postulate constantly when cutting materials, laying out foundations, and assembling structures.

examples.explanation: A carpenter needs to cut a board 12 feet long into two pieces. If the first piece is 5 feet, the second must be 12 - 5 = 7 feet. This is the Segment Addition Postulate: total length equals part 1 plus part 2.

image.alt: A 12-foot board shown as a segment. A cut point divides it into a 5-foot piece and a 7-foot piece. Equation: 5 + 7 = 12. Saw icon at the cut point.
image.caption: Cutting lumber: The total board length equals the sum of the cut pieces.

examples.explanation: The total fence length is the sum of the individual side segments. If three sides of a rectangular yard measure 40 ft, 60 ft, and 40 ft, the fourth side must be 60 ft to close the rectangle.

image.alt: Top-down view of a rectangular yard. Three sides labeled: 40 ft, 60 ft, 40 ft. The fourth side has a question mark then '= 60 ft'. Total perimeter = 200 ft.
image.caption: Fencing: Each side is a segment; opposite sides of a rectangle are congruent.

categories.description: Distances along routes are calculated using segment addition principles. Maps and GPS systems treat paths as sequences of connected segments.

examples.explanation: The distance from City A to City C via City B equals the distance from A to B plus the distance from B to C. GPS systems sum segment distances to calculate total route length.

image.alt: A road map segment: City A to City B = 120 miles, City B to City C = 85 miles. Total: A to C = 120 + 85 = 205 miles. The road is shown as connected segments.
image.caption: Road trips: Total distance is the sum of segment distances along the route.

examples.explanation: A trail is marked with distance posts. Post 1 at mile 0, post 2 at mile 3.7, post 3 at mile 8.2. Distance from post 2 to post 3 = 8.2 - 3.7 = 4.5 miles (subtracting segment positions).

image.alt: A winding trail with three posts: Post 1 (0 mi), Post 2 (3.7 mi), Post 3 (8.2 mi). Distance between posts 2 and 3 calculated as 8.2 - 3.7 = 4.5 miles. Segment highlighted.
image.caption: Trail markers: Distance between posts is the difference of their positions.

categories.description: Interior design, graphic design, and page layout involve dividing spaces into segments and using midpoints for centering and balance.

examples.explanation: A wall is 16 feet long. To center a 6-foot sofa, find the midpoint of the wall (8 feet from either end), then place the sofa's midpoint there. The sofa extends 3 feet on each side of the midpoint.

image.alt: A 16-foot wall shown as a segment. Midpoint at 8 feet. A 6-foot sofa centered: extends from 5 ft to 11 ft (3 ft on each side of the midpoint). Measurements labeled clearly.
image.caption: Centering: The midpoint of the wall aligns with the midpoint of the sofa.

examples.explanation: A page is 24 picas wide. Dividing it into three equal columns means each column is 8 picas wide. The midpoints of columns determine text alignment. This is repeated segment division into congruent parts.

image.alt: A 24-pica wide page divided into three 8-pica columns. Each column's midpoint marked. Gutters between columns shown as small segments. Total = 8 + 8 + 8 = 24 picas.
image.caption: Page layout: The total width equals the sum of column widths plus gutters.

sections.introduction: Mastery of segment measurement requires practice with notation, the Segment Addition Postulate, midpoints, and algebraic problem-solving. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Segment Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) B is between A and C. AB = 8, BC = 13. Find AC.
2) B is between A and C. AC = 35, AB = 17. Find BC.
3) B is between A and C. AB = 3x - 2, BC = 2x + 5, AC = 6x - 1. Find x and all lengths.
4) M is the midpoint of PQ—. PM = 4x + 1, MQ = 7x - 8. Find x, PM, MQ, and PQ.
5) In the diagram, AB— ≅ BC— and BC— ≅ CD—. If AD = 36, find AB.

step_by_step.explanation:
AB— ≅ BC— ≅ CD— means AB = BC = CD.
Points A, B, C, D are collinear and in order.
AD = AB + BC + CD = AB + AB + AB = 3(AB)
36 = 3(AB) → AB = 12

image.alt: Five diagrams corresponding to the five practice problems: (1) AB = 8, BC = 13, AC = 21. (2) AC = 35, AB = 17, BC = 18. (3) Algebraic expressions with solution. (4) Midpoint with PM = MQ = 13. (5) Chain of three congruent segments, AB = 12.
image.caption: Practice problems: Applying the Segment Addition Postulate, midpoint condition, and congruence.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Segment Addition Postulate, midpoint condition (AM = MB), congruent segments, algebraic equation solving, and multi-segment chains.

summary: Line segments are finite, measurable portions of lines bounded by two endpoints. Key points: 1) Notation: AB— refers to the segment (the geometric figure); AB refers to its length (a number). Congruent segments (AB— ≅ CD—) have equal length (AB = CD). 2) Segment Addition Postulate: If point B lies between points A and C, then AB + BC = AC. The whole length equals the sum of its parts. This requires collinearity and betweenness. 3) Midpoint: The point M that divides a segment into two congruent segments. If M is the midpoint of AB—, then AM = MB, and AM + MB = AB, so AM = MB = AB ÷ 2. 4) Segment Bisector: Any point, line, ray, or plane that passes through the midpoint of a segment. The perpendicular bisector is a special case that also forms a 90° angle. 5) Algebraic applications: The Segment Addition Postulate and midpoint condition are used to set up equations when lengths are expressed with variables. Always verify that final lengths are positive. 6) Common errors: Confusing segment notation (bar vs. no bar), applying the postulate without collinearity, misidentifying the midpoint condition, and accepting negative lengths. Mastering segments and length measurement is foundational for perimeter, coordinate geometry, and all subsequent geometry topics.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Line Segments' in center. Branches: Notation (AB— = segment, AB = length, ≅ = congruence), Segment Addition Postulate (AB + BC = AC when B is between A and C), Midpoint (AM = MB = AB/2), Bisectors (any figure through midpoint, perpendicular bisector special case). Applications: algebraic equations, construction, navigation, design layout.
review_questions_image.caption: Segments and Length Review: A visual concept map connecting notation, the Segment Addition Postulate, midpoints, and bisectors.
